question:
Let $n$ be an even positive integer.  Write the numbers
$1,2,\ldots,n^2$ in the squares of an $n\times n$ grid so that the
$k$-th row, from left to right, is
\[(k-1)n+1,(k-1)n+2,\ldots, (k-1)n+n.\]
Color the squares of the grid so that half of the squares in each
row and in each column are red and the other half are black (a
checkerboard coloring is one possibility).  Prove that for each
coloring, the sum of the numbers on the red squares is equal to
the sum of the numbers on the black squares.
solution:
Let $R$ (resp.\ $B$) denote the set of red (resp.\ black) squares in
such a coloring, and for $s\in R\cup B$, let $f(s)n+g(s)+1$ denote the
number written in square $s$, where $0\leq f(s),g(s)\leq n-1$.
Then it is clear that the value of $f(s)$ depends only on the row of
$s$, while the value of $g(s)$ depends only on the column of $s$.  Since
every row contains exactly $n/2$ elements of $R$ and $n/2$ elements of $B$,
\[ \sum_{s\in R} f(s) = \sum_{s\in B} f(s) .\]
Similarly, because every column contains exactly $n/2$ elements of $R$ and
$n/2$ elements of $B$,
\[ \sum_{s\in R} g(s) = \sum_{s\in B} g(s) .\]
It follows that
\[\sum_{s\in R} f(s)n+g(s)+1 = \sum_{s\in B} f(s)n+g(s)+1,\]
as desired.

Note: Richard Stanley points out a theorem of Ryser (see Ryser,
\textit{Combinatorial Mathematics}, Theorem~3.1) that can also be applied.
Namely, if $A$ and $B$ are $0-1$ matrices with the same row and column
sums, then there is a sequence of operations on $2 \times 2$ matrices
of the form
\[
\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \to
\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
\]
or vice versa, which transforms $A$ into $B$. If we identify 0 and 1 with
red and black, then the given coloring and the checkerboard coloring
both satisfy the sum condition. Since the desired result is clearly
true for the checkerboard coloring, and performing the matrix operations
does not affect this, the desired result follows in general.

descriptive_long.question:
Let $gridsize$ be an even positive integer.  Write the numbers
$1,2,\ldots,gridsize^2$ in the squares of an $gridsize\times gridsize$ grid so that the
$rowind$-th row, from left to right, is
\[(rowind-1)gridsize+1,(rowind-1)gridsize+2,\ldots, (rowind-1)gridsize+gridsize.\]
Color the squares of the grid so that half of the squares in each
row and in each column are red and the other half are black (a
checkerboard coloring is one possibility).  Prove that for each
coloring, the sum of the numbers on the red squares is equal to
the sum of the numbers on the black squares.
descriptive_long.solution:
Let $redset$ (resp.\ $blackset$) denote the set of red (resp.\ black) squares in
such a coloring, and for $squares\in redset\cup blackset$, let $rowfunc(squares)gridsize+colfunc(squares)+1$ denote the
number written in square $squares$, where $0\leq rowfunc(squares),colfunc(squares)\leq gridsize-1$.
Then it is clear that the value of $rowfunc(squares)$ depends only on the row of
$squares$, while the value of $colfunc(squares)$ depends only on the column of $squares$.  Since
every row contains exactly $gridsize/2$ elements of $redset$ and $gridsize/2$ elements of $blackset$,
\[ \sum_{squares\in redset} rowfunc(squares) = \sum_{squares\in blackset} rowfunc(squares) .\]
Similarly, because every column contains exactly $gridsize/2$ elements of $redset$ and
$gridsize/2$ elements of $blackset$,
\[ \sum_{squares\in redset} colfunc(squares) = \sum_{squares\in blackset} colfunc(squares) .\]
It follows that
\[\sum_{squares\in redset} rowfunc(squares)gridsize+colfunc(squares)+1 = \sum_{squares\in blackset} rowfunc(squares)gridsize+colfunc(squares)+1,\]
as desired.

Note: Richard Stanley points out a theorem of Ryser (see Ryser,
\textit{Combinatorial Mathematics}, Theorem~3.1) that can also be applied.
Namely, if $matrixa$ and $blackset$ are $0-1$ matrices with the same row and column
sums, then there is a sequence of operations on $2 \times 2$ matrices
of the form
\[
\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \to
\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
\]
or vice versa, which transforms $matrixa$ into $blackset$. If we identify 0 and 1 with
red and black, then the given coloring and the checkerboard coloring
both satisfy the sum condition. Since the desired result is clearly
true for the checkerboard coloring, and performing the matrix operations
does not affect this, the desired result follows in general.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $periwinks$ be an even positive integer.  Write the numbers
$1,2,\ldots,periwinks^2$ in the squares of an $periwinks\times periwinks$ grid so that the
$marzipans$-th row, from left to right, is
\[(marzipans-1)periwinks+1,(marzipans-1)periwinks+2,\ldots, (marzipans-1)periwinks+periwinks.\]
Color the squares of the grid so that half of the squares in each
row and in each column are red and the other half are black (a
checkerboard coloring is one possibility).  Prove that for each
coloring, the sum of the numbers on the red squares is equal to
the sum of the numbers on the black squares.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Let $coppervet$ (resp.\ $almondfig$) denote the set of red (resp.\ black) squares in
such a coloring, and for $tumblebee\in coppervet\cup almondfig$, let $dandelion(tumblebee)periwinks+porcupine(tumblebee)+1$ denote the
number written in square $tumblebee$, where $0\leq dandelion(tumblebee),porcupine(tumblebee)\leq periwinks-1$.
Then it is clear that the value of $dandelion(tumblebee)$ depends only on the row of
$tumblebee$, while the value of $porcupine(tumblebee)$ depends only on the column of $tumblebee$.  Since
every row contains exactly $periwinks/2$ elements of $coppervet$ and $periwinks/2$ elements of $almondfig$,
\[ \sum_{tumblebee\in coppervet} dandelion(tumblebee) = \sum_{tumblebee\in almondfig} dandelion(tumblebee) .\]
Similarly, because every column contains exactly $periwinks/2$ elements of $coppervet$ and
$periwinks/2$ elements of $almondfig$,
\[ \sum_{tumblebee\in coppervet} porcupine(tumblebee) = \sum_{tumblebee\in almondfig} porcupine(tumblebee) .\]
It follows that
\[\sum_{tumblebee\in coppervet} dandelion(tumblebee)periwinks+porcupine(tumblebee)+1 = \sum_{tumblebee\in almondfig} dandelion(tumblebee)periwinks+porcupine(tumblebee)+1,\]
as desired.

Note: Richard Stanley points out a theorem of Ryser (see Ryser,
\textit{Combinatorial Mathematics}, Theorem~3.1) that can also be applied.
Namely, if $nectarine$ and $almondfig$ are $0-1$ matrices with the same row and column
sums, then there is a sequence of operations on $2 \times 2$ matrices
of the form
\[
\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \to
\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
\]
or vice versa, which transforms $nectarine$ into $almondfig$. If we identify 0 and 1 with
red and black, then the given coloring and the checkerboard coloring
both satisfy the sum condition. Since the desired result is clearly
true for the checkerboard coloring, and performing the matrix operations
does not affect this, the desired result follows in general.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $oddinteger$ be an even positive integer.  Write the numbers
$1,2,\ldots,oddinteger^2$ in the squares of an $oddinteger\times oddinteger$ grid so that the
$columnidx$-th row, from left to right, is
\[(columnidx-1)oddinteger+1,(columnidx-1)oddinteger+2,\ldots, (columnidx-1)oddinteger+oddinteger.\]
Color the squares of the grid so that half of the squares in each
row and in each column are red and the other half are black (a
checkerboard coloring is one possibility).  Prove that for each
coloring, the sum of the numbers on the red squares is equal to
the sum of the numbers on the black squares.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Let $greensquares$ (resp.\ $whitesquares$) denote the set of red (resp.\ black) squares in
such a coloring, and for $circleshape\in greensquares\cup whitesquares$, let $columnmap(circleshape)oddinteger+rowmapping(circleshape)+1$ denote the
number written in square $circleshape$, where $0\leq columnmap(circleshape),rowmapping(circleshape)\leq oddinteger-1$.
Then it is clear that the value of $columnmap(circleshape)$ depends only on the row of
$circleshape$, while the value of $rowmapping(circleshape)$ depends only on the column of $circleshape$.  Since
every row contains exactly $oddinteger/2$ elements of $greensquares$ and $oddinteger/2$ elements of $whitesquares$,
\[ \sum_{circleshape\in greensquares} columnmap(circleshape) = \sum_{circleshape\in whitesquares} columnmap(circleshape) .\]
Similarly, because every column contains exactly $oddinteger/2$ elements of $greensquares$ and
$oddinteger/2$ elements of $whitesquares$,
\[ \sum_{circleshape\in greensquares} rowmapping(circleshape) = \sum_{circleshape\in whitesquares} rowmapping(circleshape) .\]
It follows that
\[\sum_{circleshape\in greensquares} columnmap(circleshape)oddinteger+rowmapping(circleshape)+1 = \sum_{circleshape\in whitesquares} columnmap(circleshape)oddinteger+rowmapping(circleshape)+1,\]
as desired.

Note: Richard Stanley points out a theorem of Ryser (see Ryser,
\textit{Combinatorial Mathematics}, Theorem~3.1) that can also be applied.
Namely, if $vectorfield$ and $whitesquares$ are $0-1$ matrices with the same row and column
sums, then there is a sequence of operations on $2 \times 2$ matrices
of the form
\[
\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \to
\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
\]
or vice versa, which transforms $vectorfield$ into $whitesquares$. If we identify 0 and 1 with
red and black, then the given coloring and the checkerboard coloring
both satisfy the sum condition. Since the desired result is clearly
true for the checkerboard coloring, and performing the matrix operations
does not affect this, the desired result follows in general.

garbled_string.question:
Let $zmbvtcqe$ be an even positive integer.  Write the numbers
$1,2,\ldots,zmbvtcqe^2$ in the squares of an $zmbvtcqe\times zmbvtcqe$ grid so that the
$xlprmnoq$-th row, from left to right, is
\[(xlprmnoq-1)zmbvtcqe+1,(xlprmnoq-1)zmbvtcqe+2,\ldots, (xlprmnoq-1)zmbvtcqe+zmbvtcqe.\]
Color the squares of the grid so that half of the squares in each
row and in each column are red and the other half are black (a
checkerboard coloring is one possibility).  Prove that for each
coloring, the sum of the numbers on the red squares is equal to
the sum of the numbers on the black squares.
garbled_string.solution:
Let $peojxkaw$ (resp.\ $mwytrzls$) denote the set of red (resp.\ black) squares in
such a coloring, and for $dqjsklwe\in peojxkaw\cup mwytrzls$, let $udbqnoas(dqjsklwe)zmbvtcqe+achvrpmt(dqjsklwe)+1$ denote the
number written in square $dqjsklwe$, where $0\leq udbqnoas(dqjsklwe),achvrpmt(dqjsklwe)\leq zmbvtcqe-1$.
Then it is clear that the value of $udbqnoas(dqjsklwe)$ depends only on the row of
$dqjsklwe$, while the value of $achvrpmt(dqjsklwe)$ depends only on the column of $dqjsklwe$.  Since
every row contains exactly $zmbvtcqe/2$ elements of $peojxkaw$ and $zmbvtcqe/2$ elements of $mwytrzls$,
\[ \sum_{dqjsklwe\in peojxkaw} udbqnoas(dqjsklwe) = \sum_{dqjsklwe\in mwytrzls} udbqnoas(dqjsklwe) .\]
Similarly, because every column contains exactly $zmbvtcqe/2$ elements of $peojxkaw$ and
$zmbvtcqe/2$ elements of $mwytrzls$,
\[ \sum_{dqjsklwe\in peojxkaw} achvrpmt(dqjsklwe) = \sum_{dqjsklwe\in mwytrzls} achvrpmt(dqjsklwe) .\]
It follows that
\[\sum_{dqjsklwe\in peojxkaw} udbqnoas(dqjsklwe)zmbvtcqe+achvrpmt(dqjsklwe)+1 = \sum_{dqjsklwe\in mwytrzls} udbqnoas(dqjsklwe)zmbvtcqe+achvrpmt(dqjsklwe)+1,\]
as desired.

Note: Richard Stanley points out a theorem of Ryser (see Ryser,
\textit{Combinatorial Mathematics}, Theorem~3.1) that can also be applied.
Namely, if $vzxulnid$ and $mwytrzls$ are $0-1$ matrices with the same row and column
sums, then there is a sequence of operations on $2 \times 2$ matrices
of the form
\[
\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \to
\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
\]
or vice versa, which transforms $vzxulnid$ into $mwytrzls$. If we identify 0 and 1 with
red and black, then the given coloring and the checkerboard coloring
both satisfy the sum condition. Since the desired result is clearly
true for the checkerboard coloring, and performing the matrix operations
does not affect this, the desired result follows in general.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $m$ be a positive integer and set $n=2m$.  In the $n\times n$ array whose rows are numbered $0,1,\dots ,n-1$ from top to bottom and whose columns are numbered $0,1,\dots ,n-1$ from left to right, write the arithmetic progression\[
7,\;12,\;17,\;\dots ,\;5\bigl(n^{2}-1\bigr)+7
\]column-by-column, i.e.
\[
a_{r,c}=5\,(cn+r)+7\qquad(0\le r,c\le n-1).
\]
Now color each square either gold or silver in such a way that every row contains exactly $m$ gold squares and every column contains exactly $m$ gold squares (so the remaining squares in each row or column are silver).

Prove that, regardless of the particular gold/silver coloring, the sum of the numbers in the gold squares equals the sum of the numbers in the silver squares.
kernel_variant.solution:
Index the array by $(r,c)$ with $0\le r,c\le n-1$ and $n=2m$.  The entry in position $(r,c)$ is

$$a_{r,c}=5\,(cn+r)+7=(5n)c+5r+7.
$$

Let $G$ and $S$ denote the sets of gold and silver squares, respectively.  Write

$$\Delta=\sum_{(r,c)\in G}a_{r,c}-\sum_{(r,c)\in S}a_{r,c}
$$
(gold - silver).  Substituting the expression for $a_{r,c}$ gives

$$\Delta=5n\Bigl(\sum_{(r,c)\in G}c-\sum_{(r,c)\in S}c\Bigr)
+5\Bigl(\sum_{(r,c)\in G}r-\sum_{(r,c)\in S}r\Bigr)
+7\bigl(|G|-|S|\bigr).
$$

Because each row contains exactly $m=n/2$ gold squares and $m$ silver squares, the index $r$ occurs $m$ times in $G$ and $m$ times in $S$, so

$$\sum_{(r,c)\in G}r=\sum_{(r,c)\in S}r.
$$

Similarly, each column contains $m$ gold and $m$ silver squares, giving

$$\sum_{(r,c)\in G}c=\sum_{(r,c)\in S}c.
$$

Finally, there are $n^2$ squares total and each of the $n$ rows contributes $m$ gold and $m$ silver, so

$$|G|=|S|=mn=n^2/2.
$$

Hence all three differences in $\Delta$ vanish, so $\Delta=0$.  Therefore the sum of the numbers in the gold squares equals the sum of the numbers in the silver squares.

core_steps: Express the entry in square s as  nÂ·row(s) + col(s) + c  (linear in row and column).
Write the colorâ€“sum difference as  nÂ·(Î£_row_indices_red âˆ’ Î£_row_indices_black) + (Î£_col_indices_red âˆ’ Î£_col_indices_black).
Because each row contains the same number of red and black squares, the two colorâ€“row sums are equal.
Because each column contains the same number of red and black squares, the two colorâ€“column sums are equal.
Both gaps are 0 â‡’ the overall red and black sums coincide.

slot1.description: Parity requirement on the grid size (only needed so that â€˜half of the squaresâ€™ makes sense).  Any specification guaranteeing an equal split in each row/column works.
slot1.original: n is an even positive integer

slot2.description: Additive offset in the labeling formula; may be any constant because it contributes equally to both colors.

slot3.description: Common difference (scaling factor) of the arithmetic progression of labels; any positive integer keeps the proof intact.

slot4.description: Direction of the linear labeling (row-major vs column-major); either choice leaves the separation into a row term and a column term unchanged.

slot5.description: Names of the two classes; any two distinct symbols/colors suffice.
